name: Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop
name_original: Villalobar de Rioja - Castañares de Rioja - Bañares – circular desde Santo Domingo de la Calzada
description: Enjoy an easy 15.4-mile touring cycling loop through the Oja River valley, passing vineyards and the historic Santo Domingo de la Calzada Ca
region_intro: Touring cycling around Santo Domingo De La Calzada features diverse landscapes, from the Oja River valley to rolling hills dotted with vineyards. The region is characterized by the Oja Greenway, which offers accessible routes, and the broader countryside with fields of wheat and barley. To the south, the Sierra de la Demanda mountains, including peaks like San Lorenzo (2,262 m), present more challenging terrain. Many routes follow or branch off the historic Camino de Santiago, integrating cultural landmarks with natural scenery. Best touring cycling routes around Santo Domingo De La Calzada The most popular touring cycling route is Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop, a 15.4 miles (24.7 km) trail that takes 1 hour 38 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the scenic Oja River valley and its surrounding vineyards. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Anguciana – Castillo de los Velasco loop from Santo Domingo de la Calzada, a moderate 31.6 miles (50.9 km) path. This route offers varied terrain through rolling hills and countryside, passing historical sites. Local touring cyclists also love the Dangerous crossing loop from Santo Domingo de la Calzada, a 20.5 miles (33.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain and historic surroundings, often completed in about 2 hours 18 minutes. Touring cycling around Santo Domingo De La Calzada is defined by riverside paths along the Oja River, extensive vineyards, and rolling hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging rides with significant elevation gain. The routes in Santo Domingo De La Calzada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.7 stars from over 20 reviews. Nearly 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Santo Domingo De La Calzada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Santo Domingo de la Calzada?
A: There are nearly 30 touring cycling routes around Santo Domingo de la Calzada, offering a variety of options for different skill levels and preferences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.7 stars from over 20 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Santo Domingo de la Calzada?
A: The terrain varies significantly. You'll find easy, mostly paved routes along the Oja River valley, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995661460/santo-domingo-de-la-calzada-and-via-verde-del-oja-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which passes through vineyards. Other routes feature rolling hills and countryside, characteristic of northern Spain, with some unpaved or gravel sections, especially as you head towards the Sierra de la Demanda mountains to the south.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are several easy routes perfect for beginners and families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995661460/santo-domingo-de-la-calzada-and-via-verde-del-oja-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice, covering 24.7 km through the scenic Oja River valley with minimal elevation gain. This route is mostly flat and accessible, ideal for a relaxed ride.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1143859" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic streets of Belorado – Belorado loop from Santo Domingo de la Calzada&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain and longer distances, reaching over 80 km. The region also features routes that venture into the Sierra de la Demanda mountains, providing more rugged landscapes and demanding climbs.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes either follow or branch off the historic Camino de Santiago, allowing you to explore rich cultural heritage. You can encounter impressive structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/236370" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de la Calzada Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, the medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/655221" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Oja River&lt;/a&gt;, and remnants of 14th-century city walls. Natural features include the picturesque Oja River valley, extensive Rioja vineyards, and rolling hills.

Q: Are there any castles or historical sites to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in historical sites. You can find several castles, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5557168" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leiva Castle&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4705240" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de los Velasco&lt;/a&gt;, which can be seen on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17544452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Anguciana – Castillo de los Velasco loop from Santo Domingo de la Calzada&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable sites include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2918989" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Millán de la Cogolla Monastery&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bicigrino Statue&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Santo Domingo de la Calzada?
A: The best time for touring cycling is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) when the weather is mild and pleasant. These seasons offer comfortable temperatures for riding and showcase the region's natural beauty, from blooming vineyards to vibrant autumn foliage. Summers can be hot, while winters are cooler and may have more rainfall.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Santo Domingo de la Calzada are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995661460/santo-domingo-de-la-calzada-and-via-verde-del-oja-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40316299" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dangerous crossing loop from Santo Domingo de la Calzada&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is it possible to bring my dog on touring cycling routes in this region?
A: While many natural areas in La Rioja are generally dog-friendly, it's always best to check specific regulations for each route or protected area. The Vía Verde del Oja, for example, is often accessible for dogs on a leash. However, be mindful of livestock in rural areas and ensure your dog is comfortable with cycling speeds and distances.

Q: Are there options for parking near the touring cycling routes?
A: Santo Domingo de la Calzada, being a hub for pilgrims and tourists, offers various parking options within and around the town. Many routes start directly from the town center, making it convenient to park your vehicle and begin your ride. Look for public parking areas, especially near the historic center or the outskirts of town.

Q: Can I access touring cycling routes using public transport?
A: Santo Domingo de la Calzada is connected by regional bus services, which may offer limited options for transporting bicycles. However, it's advisable to check with the specific bus operator regarding their bicycle policies and space availability, especially during peak seasons. The town's central location makes it a good starting point for routes, reducing the need for extensive public transport use once you arrive.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the routes?
A: Given its location on the Camino de Santiago, Santo Domingo de la Calzada and the surrounding villages offer numerous opportunities for refreshments and accommodation. You'll find cafes, restaurants, and traditional 'paradores' (like the Old Pilgrims' Hospital) in the town itself, and smaller eateries in the villages along the routes. Many routes pass through or near these villages, providing convenient stops.
tips:
Nice bridge of masonry stone that was built in the 11th century for the passage of pilgrims.
At the entrance, on the banks of Santo Domingo de la Calzada, there is a small hermitage and, behind it, a picnic area with two tables.
In the Rioja Alta, the grapevine characterizes the area. Everywhere you can see vineyards, which are on barren, stony soil. This obviously results in the good taste of the Rioja.
Monumental Gothic temple built between 1490 and 1510 in the town of Bañares, in the region of Santo Domingo de la Calzada (Rioja Alta).

In addition to the fact that it is the only church-fortress in La Rioja, what is most striking is that inside it houses a henhouse in which a real rooster and hen live, which are also protected as an Asset of Cultural Interest by the Government of La Rioja.
The animals are replaced every two weeks and allude to the legend of the hanged man. The myth tells that a pilgrim was hanged after being unjustly accused of theft. When his parents went to see the body they found their son alive and he told them that Santo Domingo de la Calzada had saved his life. The parents proclaimed that their son was alive, but the mayor of the city did not believe them and told them something like: "Your son is as alive as the roasted rooster and hen that I am eating." And suddenly, according to legend, the animals regained their feathers and came back to life.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Santo Domingo de la Calzada and Vía Verde del Oja Loop?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde del Oja, which forms a significant part of this loop, features a mixed surface. You'll primarily ride on compacted earth and gravel paths, with some sections also being asphalted. This makes for a smooth and stable ride, well-suited for touring bikes.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! This route is considered very easy and accessible. It has a 'white' difficulty rating and a 'green' climb difficulty, with a very gentle average gradient of 0.9% to 1.3%. This makes it perfect for cyclists of all fitness levels, including families and those new to touring.

faqs.question: Where can I park my car when cycling this loop?
faqs.answer: For cyclists starting in Santo Domingo de la Calzada, there are various parking options available within the town. Look for public parking areas, especially near the historic center or the access points to the Vía Verde, which are generally well-marked.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde del Oja and surrounding areas are enjoyable for cycling throughout much of the year. Spring (April-June) and Autumn (September-October) offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter winter months.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Vía Verde del Oja?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on the Vía Verde del Oja. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in sections where you might encounter other cyclists, pedestrians, or wildlife, and to clean up after them.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay an entrance fee to cycle the Vía Verde del Oja or the local roads that make up this loop. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: What interesting sights will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a blend of historical and natural sights. You'll pass by the historic Santo Domingo de la Calzada Bridge and the impressive Santo Domingo de la Calzada Cathedral. You'll also cycle through scenic agricultural fields, poplar groves, and sections with Vineyards as far as the eye can see, and potentially see the Church of the Holy Cross of Bañares.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Vía Verde del Oja notably intersects with the famous Camino de Santiago (French Way) in Santo Domingo de la Calzada. The route also passes through sections of the Camino de Santiago Vasco del Interior and the Vía Verde del Río Oja.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the loop?
faqs.answer: Santo Domingo de la Calzada, being a significant town and pilgrimage stop, offers numerous cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can find refreshments and meals. There are also smaller villages along the Vía Verde where you might find local establishments.

faqs.question: Is it possible to rent a bike in Santo Domingo de la Calzada?
faqs.answer: Yes, Santo Domingo de la Calzada is a popular starting point for cyclists, and you can find bike rental services in town. It's advisable to check with local tourism offices or search online for current rental options and availability, especially if you need a specific type of bike.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse and appealing scenery. The route follows the Oja River valley, transitioning from agricultural plains with cereal, potato, and beet fields to poplar groves and vineyards. Closer to the foothills of the Sierra de la Demanda, you might see forests of oak, beech, and pine.

faqs.question: What makes this route unique or historically significant?
faqs.answer: This route is unique because the Vía Verde del Oja repurposes an old narrow-gauge railway line that operated from 1916 to 1969, offering a glimpse into the region's industrial past. It also famously intersects with the Camino de Santiago in Santo Domingo de la Calzada, a historic town founded to assist pilgrims, adding a rich cultural layer to your ride.
